question.text: Scenario: Mr. Rahul Mehta, aged 35, earns Rs. 1,20,000 per month (net take-home). He has the following outstanding debts: (1) Home loan: Rs. 35 lakh outstanding at 8.5% p.a., EMI Rs. 32,000, remaining tenure 15 years; (2) Car loan: Rs. 4,50,000 outstanding at 11% p.a., EMI Rs. 10,000, remaining tenure 5 years; (3) Personal loan: Rs. 2,00,000 outstanding at 16% p.a., EMI Rs. 6,500, remaining tenure 3 years; (4) Credit card outstanding: Rs. 80,000 at 42% p.a. (revolving credit), minimum payment Rs. 4,000/month. His total monthly expenses (excluding debt servicing) are Rs. 45,000. He has recently received an annual bonus of Rs. 3,00,000 and wants advice on how to deploy it. His savings bank balance is Rs. 1,50,000 and he has Rs. 2,00,000 in a liquid mutual fund. Question 3 of 5: If Rahul uses Rs. 80,000 from the bonus to clear the credit card debt and Rs. 2,00,000 to clear the personal loan, his monthly debt servicing drops to:

explanation.text: **The Logic:** After clearing credit card (saves Rs. 4,000/month) and personal loan (saves Rs. 6,500/month): Remaining monthly debt = 32,000 (home) + 10,000 (car) = Rs. 42,000. Savings = Rs. 10,500/month. New DTI = 42,000/1,20,000 = 35%. **The Trap:** Rs. 32,000 only counts the home loan — the car loan is still outstanding. **Cross-Exam Context:** Impact of debt repayment on DTI is a practical calculation tested in X-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** By deploying Rs. 2.8 lakh of the bonus, Rahul reduces DTI from 43.8% to 35% and saves Rs. 10,500/month — these freed-up funds can now go toward investments.
